pages.full_text:
St Hilaire's church with a brightly tiled roof was originally built 1178-1230. St Hilaire was a monk born 401, who became a bishop. It has lots of interesting carvings.

people have to drive 40 minutes away to a Baptism at Gray. v. sad.

The campsite is la Colombière. It has a noisy motor + a v. noisy invisible duck. A small round blue bug eating hazel leaves + wood peckers. We had 3 walks in the interesting town. It started raining at 5 pm + rained all night. Thunderstorm.

[glued-in tourist postcards: Porte St-Hilaire — "En sortant de l'église par la porte latérale, vous arrivez sur une place où vous pouvez admirer la magnifique porte Saint-Hilaire par laquelle les soldats français de Louis XI sont entrés dans la ville en 1477." / Grande rue — "La Grande rue est le lien entre le château et l'église, où siégeaient la halle et les principales boutiques. De chaque côté, de belles et grandes maisons laissent admirer d'immenses toits pentus couverts de petites tuiles. Chacune possède une cave voûtée et un escalier de pierre en colimaçon."]
pages.notes: Two tourist postcards/leaflet pages glued in: one showing Porte St-Hilaire (an archway), one showing the Grande rue with church and buildings. The handwritten text sits to the right of and below the postcards. 'la Colombière' is the campsite name. 'Gray' appears to be a town name (possibly Gray, Haute-Saône, France).

pages.full_text:
Monday

As we reached Saline Royale it started to rain. The buildings were designed by Claude-Nicolas Ledoux (1736-1806) Soon after opening after 1793, it was privatised.

The salt factory was originally la Saline les Bains, but all the wood got used up. So they opened this in a field. Buildings well-spaced for healthy air + fire prevention. This is in a huge forest. They piped salty water from the original site to the new factory, 13k as crow flies but 33 k of 2 underground wood pipes, later changed to metal. (or ceramic?) 4 ovens heated the water.

[glued-in UNESCO postcard/brochure: PATRIMOINE MONDIAL DE L'UNESCO — photograph of the main building of Saline Royale]

[glued-in floor plan/map of Saline Royale with handwritten annotations: "4 saltovens", "A salt oven", "Living qua[rters]", "barrel-makers", "xxx a garde [?]", "for workers"]
pages.notes: A UNESCO postcard showing the main building of the Saline Royale (Royal Saltworks) is glued in, along with a printed floor plan/site map with handwritten annotations added by the diarist labelling various buildings (salt ovens, barrel-makers, living quarters, guards). Claude-Nicolas Ledoux's dates given as (1736-1806). 'Saline les Bains' may be an alternative/earlier name — could be 'Salins-les-Bains'. The year cannot be pinned down from this page alone; volume B covers 2003-2019. The handwritten annotations on the map include: '4 saltovens', 'A salt oven', 'Living qua[rters]', 'barrel-makers', 'xxx a garde [?]' with further note 'for workers'.
